section.title: Greeting from the Elder
section.sectionRationale: The opening of the letter sets its whole key. In three verses the elder names his readers, roots his affection in the truth, and pronounces a blessing that binds truth and love together. Everything that follows, the charge to love and the warning against deceivers, grows from this ground.
section.thesis: The elder loves the chosen lady and her children in the truth, a truth that abides in every believer forever, and he blesses them with grace, mercy, and peace that come from the Father and the Son and are known in truth and love.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the writer and his readers; the truth that binds them; the blessing of grace, mercy, and peace.

units.label: The elder to the chosen lady

verses.text: The elder, To the {{eklekte|chosen}} lady and her children, whom I love in the {{aletheia|truth}}, and not I alone, but also all who know the truth,
because of the truth that abides in us and will be with us forever:
{{charis|Grace}}, mercy, and peace from God the Father and from Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father, will be with us in truth and {{agape|love}}.

eklekte.gloss: Chosen, elect, selected by God. The lady is addressed as chosen, echoing how the whole church is called God's elect. Whether the phrase names a particular Christian woman and her family or a local congregation and its members, the word grounds the readers' identity not in their own standing but in God's gracious choosing.

aletheia.gloss: Truth, reality, what is genuine and trustworthy. The word rings through this short letter, and here it is the very atmosphere of the elder's love: he loves them in the truth. Truth in John is not only right doctrine but the reality of God revealed in Christ, embraced and lived. It is the shared ground on which all who know God stand together.

charis.gloss: Grace, undeserved favour, God's kindness freely given. It heads the apostolic blessing, followed by mercy and peace. Grace is the fountain: God's unearned goodness in Christ from which mercy toward the needy and peace with God and one another both flow.

agape.gloss: Love, self-giving devotion that seeks another's good. Paired here with truth as the setting in which grace, mercy, and peace are enjoyed. For John, truth and love are never rivals; the same God gives both, and His people are meant to hold them together.

basic.context: This is the greeting of a very short, personal letter. An older leader, who simply calls himself the elder, writes to a Christian woman and her children, or perhaps to a whole church pictured as a lady and her members. He tells them he loves them, and that his love is bound up with the truth they share. Then he prays a blessing over them.
basic.hermeneutics: A New Testament greeting is more than a polite hello; it announces the letter's themes. Here the twin notes of truth and love sound at once, and they will run through the whole letter. The blessing is a real prayer, not a formality, and it names where grace, mercy, and peace come from: the Father and His Son.
basic.originalLanguages: The readers are the chosen, eklekte, an echo of God choosing His people. The word truth, aletheia, appears again and again in these verses; it is the reality of God in Christ, shared by all who know Him. The blessing is grace, charis, then mercy and peace, all enjoyed in truth and love, agape.
basic.typology: There is no formal type here, but the pattern is rich: God the Father and Jesus Christ the Son are named together as the one source of blessing, an early and clear witness to who Jesus is. The truth that abides forever points to the permanence of what God has done in Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus Christ is named as the Son of the Father, the joint source, with the Father, of grace, mercy, and peace. To place the Son beside the Father as the giver of divine blessing is to confess His deity. He is the truth in which the elder loves, and love and truth meet in Him.
basic.god: God is the Father who chooses a people and pours out grace, mercy, and peace. He is not distant but gives Himself, and He gives truth and love together. What God joins, His people must not tear apart.
basic.crossReferences: 1 John opens with the same union of truth and love. 3 John, also from the elder, greets a friend loved in the truth. Paul's letters open with grace and peace from the Father and the Son, the same confession of who Jesus is.
basic.oneStory: The letter opens where the whole Bible story arrives: a people chosen by God, loved in the truth, blessed by the Father and the Son. Truth and love, so often split apart in the world, are here held together in Christ, and that union is the ground of everything the elder will go on to say.

context.literary: The standard opening of an ancient letter, sender to recipient with a greeting, but filled with John's vocabulary. Truth appears four times in the first three verses, and love frames the whole. The greeting previews the letter's two great concerns, walking in love and holding to the truth.
context.historical: The elder is almost certainly the apostle John, writing near the end of the first century to a household or a congregation under his care. Travelling teachers moved between such house churches, some faithful and some not, which is why truth is stressed from the very first line.
context.canonical: One of three short letters traditionally ascribed to John, closely tied to 1 John and 3 John in language and theme. The pairing of truth and love here belongs to the same witness that runs through the Gospel of John and the first letter.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To greet the readers warmly, to establish that his love for them is grounded in shared truth, and to bless them from the Father and the Son, setting the tone for the appeal and warning that follow.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greeting mainly describes a relationship and pronounces a blessing. Its abiding claim is prescriptive in effect: God's people are to be known by truth and love held together, and their identity rests on God's choosing, not their own merit.

debates.question: Who is the chosen lady and her children?

views.label: A specific Christian woman and her family
views.case: The letter reads naturally as personal, addressed to a real believing woman and her actual children, some of whom the elder has met walking in the truth.

views.label: A local church and its members
views.case: The lady may be a congregation pictured as a woman, with her children as its members; the closing greeting from her elect sister's children would then be a sister church. The plural you in places fits a community.

debates.bearing: Either way the teaching stands: a people chosen by God, loved in the truth, and called to walk in love while guarding the truth. The letter's charge applies to households and churches alike.

study.originalLanguages: The greeting is saturated with truth, aletheia: the elder loves in the truth, all who know the truth love with him, and it is the truth that abides in us and will be with us forever. Truth here is not cold doctrine but the living reality of God in Christ, the shared ground of the family of faith. The readers are the chosen, eklekte, named for God's electing grace. The blessing runs grace, charis, then mercy and peace, and they are enjoyed in truth and love, agape, the two notes the whole letter will hold together.

typology.type: The truth that abides in us and will be with us forever
typology.fulfillment: The permanent indwelling of God's revelation in Christ, sealed by the Spirit of truth whom Jesus promised would be with His people forever, John 14:16-17.

typology.type: The Father and the Son named as one source of blessing
typology.fulfillment: The full revelation of the triune God, in whom grace, mercy, and peace have their single origin.

study.christ: Jesus Christ is named as the Son of the Father and, together with the Father, the source of grace, mercy, and peace. To set the Son beside the Father as giver of divine blessing is a plain confession of His deity, the very truth the deceivers later in the letter deny. He is also the truth in which the elder loves his readers; in Christ, love and truth are one. The abiding truth that will be with us forever is nothing less than the reality of God revealed in the Son.
study.god: God is the Father who chooses and loves a people, and who gives grace, mercy, and peace through His Son. He is a God of both truth and love, and He gives them together, never one at the expense of the other. His blessing is not earned but granted; His people are the chosen, held by His electing kindness, and the truth He gives abides with them forever.

crossReferences.note: The same author opens by uniting truth, fellowship, and love, grounding it all in the Father and the Son.
The elder again writes of one he loves in the truth, the twin sister letter to this one.
Jesus promises the Spirit of truth who will abide with His people forever, illuminating the truth that abides in us.
Grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus, the same threefold blessing confessing the Son beside the Father.

study.oneStory: The letter opens on ground the whole Bible has been moving toward: a people chosen by God, loved in the truth, and blessed by the Father together with the Son. Truth and love, so easily torn apart, are held together here in Christ. From this settled foundation the elder will call his readers to walk in love and to guard the truth, and every line assumes the God who gives both freely in His Son.

sermon.title: Truth and Love
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly on the opening of 2 John, 1:1-3, showing how the elder binds his love for the readers to the truth they share and how the letter's blessing joins truth and love in the Father and the Son. A clear, orthodox handling of this greeting.

quiz.q: How does the elder describe his love for the readers?

options.text: He loves them in the truth, together with all who know the truth.
options.note: Verse 1 grounds his love in the shared truth, joined by all who know it.

options.text: He loves them because they are his own family by birth.
options.note: There is real affection, but the text roots the love in truth, not natural kinship.

options.text: He loves them only for their usefulness to his ministry.
options.note: Nothing in the greeting reduces his love to usefulness.

options.text: He says he cannot really love people he has not met.
options.note: The opposite is true; all who know the truth share this love.

quiz.q: Where do grace, mercy, and peace come from in verse 3?

options.text: From God the Father and from Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father.
options.note: Verse 3 names the Father and the Son together as the one source.

options.text: From the Father alone, with the Son only mentioned in passing.
options.note: The Son is named as a joint source, not merely mentioned; both give the blessing.

options.text: From the elder's own authority as a leader.
options.note: The elder pronounces the blessing, but its source is God, not himself.
Grace and mercy are by definition unearned, given by God.

quiz.q: What two things does the greeting hold together?

options.text: Truth and love, named as the setting for God's blessing.
options.note: The letter's twin themes sound together from the first verses.

options.text: Truth and law, as rival demands on the reader.
options.note: Truth is central, but the pairing here is truth and love, not law, and they are not rivals.

options.text: Wealth and status, as marks of God's favour.
options.note: The greeting says nothing of wealth or status.

options.text: Fear and judgment, as the tone of the letter.
options.note: The tone is warm blessing; truth and love, not fear, frame it.

quiz.q: What does calling the readers chosen, eklekte, emphasise?

options.text: Their identity rests on God's gracious choosing, not their own merit.
options.note: The word points to God's electing grace as the ground of who they are.

options.text: That they had earned a special rank among believers.
options.note: It is about God choosing, not a rank they earned.

options.text: That only a tiny elite can belong to God.
options.note: The word describes God's people, not a narrow elite; the whole church is called elect.

options.text: That their standing depends on their family name.
options.note: Their standing rests on God's choice, not lineage.

questions: The elder loves his readers in the truth. How does sharing the truth about Jesus deepen your love for other believers, even those you have never met?
The truth abides in us and will be with us forever. What comfort is there for you in knowing that God's truth is a permanent gift, not something you must keep hold of by your own strength?
Grace, mercy, and peace come from the Father and the Son together. How does it shape your prayers to know these gifts flow from God and not from your own performance?
Truth and love are held together from the very first lines. Where are you tempted to choose one at the expense of the other, and what would it look like to hold both as this letter does?

translationNotes.note: The elder, presbyteros, likely designates the apostle John by his role and age rather than a mere office. Where the BSB prints em dashes around 'and not I alone, but also all who know the truth', they are rendered here as commas per house style.
The chosen lady, eklekte kyria, can be read as a specific believing woman or as a figure for a local church; the Greek allows both, and the letter's application holds either way.
Some manuscripts read 'the Lord Jesus Christ'; the BSB follows the reading 'Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father', which underscores His relation to the Father as joint source of the blessing.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 John 1:1-3. Em dashes in verse 1 rendered as commas per house style.
